meta.title: W204 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W204 engine — M271, M272, M276, OM646, OM651, OM642, M156. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the pre‑2009 V6 balance‑shaft risk explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes W204 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W204 engine typically costs £1,500–£12,000 supply only [MB‑QUOTE], depending on engine — the four‑cylinder diesel (OM646/OM651) runs £2,500–£5,500, while the M156 V8 in the C 63 AMG can cost £6,000–£12,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W204 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM646 2.1‑litre diesel (pre‑2010 C 200/220 CDI) is the most dependable — it’s simple, robust, and lacks the rear‑mounted timing chain of its OM651 successor. Among petrols, the post‑2011 M276 3.5‑litre V6 is a significant improvement over the balance‑shaft‑prone M272.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes W204?

acceptedAnswer.text: Pre‑2009 M272 V6 petrols suffer balance‑shaft gear wear; OM651 diesels have a rear‑mounted timing chain that can stretch; OM642 V6 diesels leak oil from the cooler seals; and M156 V8s (C 63) can develop cam lifter wear. Regular servicing is critical across all engines.

It depends on the engine and year. Pre‑facelift (2007–2010) V6 petrols have a known weakness; post‑2011 petrol V6s are much improved. The OM646 diesel is solid; OM651 needs chain monitoring. Overall, a well‑maintained W204 with a proven engine can be a reliable executive car — but do your homework.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W204 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: We don’t recommend a blanket “avoid,” but a pre‑2009 M272 V6 without documented balance‑shaft replacement is a high‑risk purchase. Similarly, an early C 63 AMG (pre‑2010) without cam lifter history should be approached with caution. Always get a specialist pre‑purchase inspection.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W204 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs include: cold‑start rattle (OM651 chain), a ticking or rattling from the front of the engine (M272 balance shaft), burning oil smell (OM642 seals), or a top‑end tick that worsens (M156 lifters). If you experience any of these, get a diagnosis immediately — early intervention saves thousands.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes W204 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £8,000 W204, a £3,000 OM651 recon is 37% of the car's value — safe if the car is otherwise sound. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon is 75% — brushing our 70% Mercedes threshold, so evaluate scrap. Use our 70% rule as your guide.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W204 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper maintenance, the OM646 diesel can exceed 250,000 miles, and the M276 V6 petrol is known to last well beyond 150,000 miles. The OM651's life is defined by the timing chain — addressed proactively, it can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles. The M156 V8 is robust but demands expensive upkeep.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W204, S204 and C204?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three share the same platform and engines. The W204 is the 4‑door saloon (2007–2014, this page), the S204 is the 5‑door estate (2008–2014), and the C204 is the 2‑door coupé (2011–2015). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W204 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and in the vehicle documentation. The code is stamped on the engine block — on OM651/OM646 it's on the front left, on M272/M276 it's on the right side. Tell us your variant and registration date, and we'll identify it for you.

hero.h1: Mercedes C‑Class W204 Engines — The Complete UK Guide

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes C‑Class W204 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W204 saloon specifically — including the pre‑facelift M272 V6 balance‑shaft risk and the post‑2011 M276 replacement.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: OM651 and OM642 ratings: 95% confidence, inherited from the Model Page where one exists. M271, M272, M276, M156, and OM646: Expert‑knowledge tier, derived from cross‑referencing specialist and owner sources — confirm against the Model Page before publishing. Data sources note: Enquiry volumes: EM_Proprietary_Data_2025.txt [MB‑VERIFIED]. Costs: Table A — Engine Replacement Economics [MB‑QUOTE]. Reliability ratings: provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any of these families; ratings should be reconciled against the published Mercedes C‑Class Model Page.

overview.intro: The W204 is the third generation of the Mercedes‑Benz C‑Class, produced from March 2007 to March 2014 as a four‑door saloon. It introduced a stiffer body shell, an extended wheelbase, and design cues from the W221 S‑Class. A significant facelift in 2011 brought new engines — including the M276 V6 replacing the M272 — and updated styling. The W204 is available with rear‑wheel drive or 4MATIC all‑wheel drive, and its engine range spans from frugal four‑cylinder diesels to the 6.2‑litre M156 V8 in the C 63 AMG.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes‑Benz W204 • Predecessor: W203 • Successor: W205 • Sibling body styles in this generation: S204 Estate (2008–2014), C204 Coupé (2011–2015).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: No W204‑specific enquiry figure is available; OM651 diesel enquiries dominate the broader Mercedes compact‑car parc, with 1,250 UK requests in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

items.quote: The 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel — 136–170 PS, simple, robust, and lacking the rear‑mounted timing chain of its OM651 successor. The OM646 is the most dependable diesel in the entire W204 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a proven, low‑maintenance W204 diesel.

items.quote: The 3.5‑litre naturally aspirated V6 — 306 PS, smooth, and a significant improvement over the balance‑shaft‑prone M272. Post‑2011 M276 V6s are the safest petrol W204s to buy.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 refinement without the M272 balance‑shaft risk.

items.quote: Pre‑2009 M272 V6 petrols carry a well‑documented balance‑shaft gear wear issue that can be catastrophic if ignored. Always verify that the balance‑shaft gear has been replaced before buying.
items.whoItsFor: buyers willing to verify full engine history before purchase.

items.quote: The 6.2‑litre AMG V8 — 457–517 PS, and a genuine performance icon. But cam lifter wear and head‑bolt issues on early cars can cost £1,500–£4,000 to fix. Budget for it.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who understand the AMG tax.

items.quote: The 2.1‑litre common‑rail diesel — 170 PS, strong economy, and the most popular W204 diesel. Provided the timing chain has been checked, this is the sweet spot of W204 ownership.
items.whoItsFor: the default choice for UK W204 buyers seeking economy and value.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Watch the Chain — stretch is common
AMG Tax — high performance, high cost
Chain Risk — rear‑mounted chain is the key
V6 Torque — smooth, but seals are labour‑intensive

economicsRule.title: The W204 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£5,500 OM651 recon against a £4,000–£8,000 W204 sits at 35–140% of the car’s value. On a sub‑£5,000 example, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we’ll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 35–70% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W204 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM646 2.1 diesel — simple, proven, cheap to fix.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W204 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M156 6.2 V8 — AMG parts and labour are eye‑wateringly expensive.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W204 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: Post‑2011 C 220 CDI (OM651) — economy and torque, provided the chain has been checked.

problems.title: M272 V6 Balance‑Shaft Gear Wear
problems.rootCause: The balance‑shaft gear on early M272 V6 engines has a soft sprocket that wears prematurely, causing the timing chain to stretch and the engine to run out of time. This is a well‑documented issue affecting pre‑2009 vehicles.
problems.recommendation: If you’re buying a pre‑2009 W204 V6, verify that the balance‑shaft gear has been replaced — a £1,200–£3,000 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500+ replacement that follows if the timing jumps. This is the single biggest buying‑guide red flag for early W204s.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your W204 →

problems.title: OM651 Timing‑Chain Stretch
problems.affectedModels: W204 C 200 CDI, C 220 CDI, C 250 CDI (OM651 diesel, 2009–2014).
problems.rootCause: The OM651 has a rear‑mounted timing chain that can stretch over time. Cold‑start rattle is the classic early warning — if ignored, the chain can jump and cause catastrophic engine damage.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold‑start rattle on an OM651, get the chain inspected immediately — a £600–£1,800 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £2,800+ replacement that follows if ignored. On a W204 worth under £5,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare OM651 engine prices for your W204 →

problems.title: OM642 Oil‑Cooler Seal Leak
problems.rootCause: The oil‑cooler O‑rings in the V of the OM642 engine degrade with heat cycling, causing oil leaks that can drip onto the exhaust and create a burning smell. Labour is intensive as the intake manifold must be removed.
problems.recommendation: If you notice a burning oil smell or oil residue in the engine V, have the oil‑cooler seals replaced immediately — an £800–£1,500 repair is cheaper than the £3,200+ replacement that follows if the engine runs low on oil.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your W204 →

problems.title: M156 Cam Lifter & Head‑Bolt Issues
problems.rootCause: Early M156 engines (2008–2010) can suffer from cam lifter wear and head‑bolt failure. Ticking from the top end is the classic warning sign — if ignored, catastrophic engine failure can occur.
problems.recommendation: If you’re buying a pre‑2010 C 63 AMG, budget for a cam lifter inspection — a £1,500–£4,000 preventative repair is a fraction of the £6,000+ replacement that follows a full failure. This is the price of AMG ownership.

cta.label: Compare M156 engine prices for your C 63 AMG →

replacementCosts.h2: W204 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

rows.variant: C 200 CDI, C 220 CDI, C 250 CDI (post‑2009)

replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page’s baseline figures — W204 shares its engine bay architecture with the S204 estate and C204 coupé, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. OM651 and OM642 costs carried forward from the EM data’s Table A.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W204 saloon only. S204 Estate and C204 Coupé share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.
